inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
I’ll build a cautious, gradual plan to get you back to half-marathon fitness without risking injury — I just need one piece of info to start. What’s your realistic weekly time availability: how many running sessions per week can you commit to, and roughly how many total training hours per week (runs + strength/cross‑training)?
If it helps, reply with one of these examples or your own numbers: “3 sessions / ~3–4 hours”, “4 sessions / ~4–6 hours”, or “5 sessions / ~6–8 hours.”
Once you send that, I’ll put together a safe 6–8 week rebuild focused on gradual mileage, two key quality sessions, and strength work — and I’ll include clear rules to avoid doing too much too soon.
outbound.html:
I’ll build a cautious, gradual plan to get you back to half-marathon fitness without risking injury — I just need one piece of info to start. What’s your realistic weekly time availability: how many running sessions per week can you commit to, and roughly how many total training hours per week (runs + strength/cross‑training)?

If it helps, reply with one of these examples or your own numbers: “3 sessions / ~3–4 hours”, “4 sessions / ~4–6 hours”, or “5 sessions / ~6–8 hours.”

Once you send that, I’ll put together a safe 6–8 week rebuild focused on gradual mileage, two key quality sessions, and strength work — and I’ll include clear rules to avoid doing too much too soon.

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
Hi there,
I’m planning a careful 6–8 week rebuild to get you back toward a half marathon this spring, with gradual mileage, two quality sessions per week, and strength work. Before I lock in the specifics, I need two details to tailor the plan safely:
- Realistic weekly running commitment: how many running sessions per week can you commit to, and roughly how many total training hours per week (runs plus strength/cross-training)?
- Target race date: which half-marathon are you aiming for, so I can align the timeline accordingly?
Quick check on the Achilles: at the moment, does the mild tightness worsen with increased load? any pain today, swelling, or new issues?
If it helps, you can mirror my examples: “3 sessions / ~3–4 hours” or “4 sessions / ~4–6 hours,” or share your own numbers.
As soon as you reply with those two pieces plus the race date, I’ll finalize a safe 6–8 week rebuild that emphasizes gradual mileage, two quality workouts per week, and supportive strength work, with clear guidelines to avoid overdoing it.
Coach
outbound.html:
Hi there,

I’m planning a careful 6–8 week rebuild to get you back toward a half marathon this spring, with gradual mileage, two quality sessions per week, and strength work. Before I lock in the specifics, I need two details to tailor the plan safely:

- Realistic weekly running commitment: how many running sessions per week can you commit to, and roughly how many total training hours per week (runs plus strength/cross-training)?
- Target race date: which half-marathon are you aiming for, so I can align the timeline accordingly?

Quick check on the Achilles: at the moment, does the mild tightness worsen with increased load? any pain today, swelling, or new issues?

If it helps, you can mirror my examples: “3 sessions / ~3–4 hours” or “4 sessions / ~4–6 hours,” or share your own numbers.

As soon as you reply with those two pieces plus the race date, I’ll finalize a safe 6–8 week rebuild that emphasizes gradual mileage, two quality workouts per week, and supportive strength work, with clear guidelines to avoid overdoing it.

Coach

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Most important: today’s session is an easy 30‑minute run — keep it very relaxed and monitor the Achilles. If you feel a meaningful increase in pain, stop and switch to walking or rest.
Plan for this week (4 sessions, controlled execution):
- Today (2026-03-17) — 30 min easy: conversational pace, stay tall, light cadence (170–180 steps/min as a guideline but don’t force it). If the Achilles is stiff, shorten to 20 min or add walking breaks.
- Strength (pick a time that avoids your busy Tuesday morning — Wed evening or Tue evening is fine) — 25–35 min: single‑leg glute bridges, bodyweight squats, calf raises (slow eccentrics: 3 sets of 8–12 slow heel drops), single‑leg balance/clock reaches, 1–2 core moves. Finish with gentle calf mobility and soft tissue work.
- Easy aerobic — midweek/after strength: 30–45 min easy, same cues as today.
- Tempo — end of week (keep it conservative): 40–50 min total with 10–15 min easy warm‑up, 15–20 min steady tempo (comfortably hard but controlled), 10 min cooldown. Reduce tempo length or convert to an easy run if the Achilles is bothered.
Key execution and safety rules (stick to these):
- Keep efforts controlled. If pain or overall fatigue increases meaningfully, downgrade that session to easy or rest. Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Maintain tall posture and light cadence; don’t force cadence changes if it increases Achilles load.
- Support training with steady sleep, simple recovery (protein + hydration), and post‑run calf mobility/icing if sore.
What I need from you after today: a quick note on how the Achilles felt during/after the 30‑minute easy run and which evening you prefer for the strength session (Wed or Tue evening). I’ll adjust the week if anything looks off.
outbound.html:
Most important: today’s session is an easy 30‑minute run — keep it very relaxed and monitor the Achilles. If you feel a meaningful increase in pain, stop and switch to walking or rest.

Plan for this week (4 sessions, controlled execution):
- Today (2026-03-17) — 30 min easy: conversational pace, stay tall, light cadence (170–180 steps/min as a guideline but don’t force it). If the Achilles is stiff, shorten to 20 min or add walking breaks.
- Strength (pick a time that avoids your busy Tuesday morning — Wed evening or Tue evening is fine) — 25–35 min: single‑leg glute bridges, bodyweight squats, calf raises (slow eccentrics: 3 sets of 8–12 slow heel drops), single‑leg balance/clock reaches, 1–2 core moves. Finish with gentle calf mobility and soft tissue work.
- Easy aerobic — midweek/after strength: 30–45 min easy, same cues as today.
- Tempo — end of week (keep it conservative): 40–50 min total with 10–15 min easy warm‑up, 15–20 min steady tempo (comfortably hard but controlled), 10 min cooldown. Reduce tempo length or convert to an easy run if the Achilles is bothered.

Key execution and safety rules (stick to these):
- Keep efforts controlled. If pain or overall fatigue increases meaningfully, downgrade that session to easy or rest. Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Maintain tall posture and light cadence; don’t force cadence changes if it increases Achilles load.
- Support training with steady sleep, simple recovery (protein + hydration), and post‑run calf mobility/icing if sore.

What I need from you after today: a quick note on how the Achilles felt during/after the 30‑minute easy run and which evening you prefer for the strength session (Wed or Tue evening). I’ll adjust the week if anything looks off.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Today’s focus is an easy 30-minute run. Keep it very relaxed. If the Achilles pain climbs beyond a mild level (2/10) or stiffness increases, stop early or switch to a walk.
This week’s four-session skeleton (cautious rebuild):
- Session 1: Today — 30-minute easy run
- Session 2: Strength, 25–35 minutes (examples: single-leg glute bridges, slow calf eccentrics, balance work). Schedule Tue evening or Wed evening; Tuesday morning is unavailable.
- Session 3: Easy aerobic, 30–45 minutes on a separate day
- Session 4: Tempo, 40–50 minutes total with 15–20 minutes at a steady tempo. If the Achilles bothers, downshift to an easy pace or walk the tempo portion.
Key guidance:
- Downgrade any session if pain or fatigue arises. Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Keep cadence light and posture tall.
- Support the work with solid sleep and simple recovery habits (calf mobility, gentle icing as needed).
Open questions to lock in the week:
- Please report how the Achilles felt during and after today’s run (pain level, stiffness, any new symptoms).
- Confirm your preferred evening for the strength session: Tue evening or Wed evening.
We’ll adapt if pain or fatigue changes. You’re aiming for the May 17 half marathon, and we’ll stay cautious to finish strong.
Best,
Your Coach
